title: त्रयोदश शिव नाम स्तोत्रम्
titleEn: Trayodash Shiv Naam (13 Names of Shiva)
devanagariText:
॥ श्री त्रयोदश शिव नाम स्तोत्रम् ॥

शिवो महेश्वरः शम्भुः पिनाकी शशिशेखरः।
वामदेवो विरूपाक्षः कपर्दी नीललोहितः॥ १॥

शङ्करः शूलपाणिश्च भवो भर्गश्च सर्वदा।
त्रयोदशैतानि नामानि प्रातरुत्थाय यः पठेत्॥ २॥

सर्वदुःखैर्विनिर्मुक्तः सर्वसिद्धिसमन्वितः।
दीर्घमायुरवाप्नोति सर्वान् कामानवाप्नुयात्॥ ३॥

शिवः — कल्याणस्वरूप
महेश्वरः — महान ईश्वर
शम्भुः — सुखदाता
पिनाकी — पिनाक धनुष धारी
शशिशेखरः — चन्द्रमा को मस्तक पर धारण करने वाले
वामदेवः — सुन्दर देव
विरूपाक्षः — विचित्र नेत्र वाले (तृतीय नेत्र)
कपर्दी — जटाधारी
नीललोहितः — नीले कण्ठ वाले
शङ्करः — शान्ति प्रदान करने वाले
शूलपाणिः — त्रिशूलधारी
भवः — संसार के स्वामी
भर्गः — पापनाशक तेज

॥ इति श्री त्रयोदश शिव नाम सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Trayodash Shiv Naam Stotram ||

Shivo Maheshvarah Shambhuh Pinaki Shashishekharah |
Vamaadevo Viroopakshah Kapardi Neelalohitah || 1 ||

Shankarah Shoolapaanishcha Bhavo Bhargashcha Sarvada |
Trayodashaitani Namani Pratar-utthaya Yah Pathet || 2 ||

Sarva-duhkhaih-vinirmuktah Sarva-siddhi-samanvitah |
Deergham-ayur-avapnoti Sarvan Kaman-avapnuyat || 3 ||

Shivah — the Auspicious One
Maheshvarah — the Great Lord
Shambhuh — the Giver of Happiness
Pinaki — Bearer of the Pinaka Bow
Shashishekharah — Moon-Crested
Vamadevah — the Beautiful God
Viroopakshah — the Three-Eyed
Kapardi — the Matted-Haired
Neelalohitah — the Blue-Throated
Shankarah — the Bestower of Peace
Shoolapaanih — the Trident-Bearer
Bhavah — the Lord of Existence
Bhargah — the Radiant Destroyer of Sin

|| Iti Shri Trayodash Shiv Naam Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning:
शिव, महेश्वर, शम्भु, पिनाकी, शशिशेखर, वामदेव, विरूपाक्ष, कपर्दी, नीललोहित, शंकर, शूलपाणि, भव और भर्ग — ये तेरह नाम हैं भगवान शिव के।

जो प्रातःकाल उठकर इन तेरह नामों का पाठ करता है, वह सर्व दुःखों से मुक्त, सर्व सिद्धियों से युक्त, दीर्घायु और सब कामनाओं को प्राप्त करने वाला होता है।
benefits: Reciting 13 names of Shiva in the morning removes all sorrows
Invokes all siddhis and fulfills all desires
Invokes the blessing of long life and perfect health
Simple yet powerful daily Shiva prayer
Referenced in Shiva Purana as essential morning practice
description: The Trayodash Shiv Naam (13 Names of Shiva), known in Sanskrit as त्रयोदश शिव नाम स्तोत्रम्, is a sacred prayer in praise of Lord Shiva. This revered text originates from Shiva Purana. Regular recitation is believed to grant all siddhis and fulfill all desires, referenced in Shiva Purana as essential morning practice, and bestow long life and perfect health. This sacred text is ideally recited on Monday (Somvar) and Daily and during Maha Shivaratri and Shravan Somvar for enhanced blessings. At 3 verses long, the recitation takes roughly 3 minutes, making it accessible for regular devotional use. On this page, you can read the complete Trayodash Shiv Naam (13 Names of Shiva) in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Trayodash Shiv Naam Stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: The Trayodash Shiv Naam Stotram (त्रयोदश शिव नाम स्तोत्रम् — Hymn of the Thirteen Names of Shiva) is a short but extremely potent 3-verse Sanskrit stotra preserved in the Shiva Purana that enumerates thirteen sacred names of Bhagavan Shiva for daily morning recitation. The thirteen names are listed in two verses: Shivo Maheshvarah Shambhuh Pinaki Shashi-shekharah, Vamadevo Virupakshah Kapardi Neelalohitah, Shankarah Shulapanish-cha Bhavo Bhargash-cha sarvada — Trayodash-aitani namani pratar-utthaya yah pathet — He who, on rising in the morning, recites these thirteen names. The third verse is the phala-shruti, promising freedom from all sorrows, attainment of every siddhi, long life and fulfilment of all desires to anyone who recites these names at dawn. The stotra is one of the shortest yet most universally chanted of all Shiva-namavalis, sometimes called the bare-essence (trayodasha-bija) of Shiva-namopasana. (Source: Shiva Purana.)

seoFaqs.question: What are the thirteen names of Shiva and their meanings?
seoFaqs.answer: The Trayodash Shiv Naam Stotram lists the thirteen names with their classical meanings: (1) Shiva — the Auspicious One, (2) Maheshvara — the Great Lord (Maha + Ishvara), (3) Shambhu — the Source of Happiness (sham — bliss + bhuh — born), (4) Pinaki — the Bearer of the Pinaka bow, (5) Shashi-shekhara — the Moon-Crested (the crescent moon on the matted hair), (6) Vamadeva — the Beautiful God (or, the West-Facing form, one of Shiva's five faces), (7) Virupaksha — the One with Strange Eyes (referring to the third eye), (8) Kapardi — the Matted-Haired Ascetic (kaparda — the conch-shaped jata), (9) Neelalohitah — the Blue-Throated Red-One (blue from Halahala poison, red from divine fire), (10) Shankara — the Bestower of Peace (sham — peace + kara — maker), (11) Shulapanih — the Trident-Bearer, (12) Bhava — the Source of All Existence, and (13) Bharga — the Radiant Destroyer of Sin (the Surya-aspect of Shiva also invoked in the Gayatri mantra Bhargo devasya dhimahi). Each name encodes one cosmic function. (Source: Trayodash Shiv Naam Stotram; Shiva Sahasranama tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: When should the Trayodash Shiv Naam be recited and what does pratar-utthaya yah pathet mean?
seoFaqs.answer: The phala-shruti specifies the recitation time precisely: pratar-utthaya yah pathet — he who recites on rising in the morning. The traditional vidhi is to chant the thirteen names immediately on waking from sleep, before stepping out of bed, with the right hand placed on the heart. This pre-bath, pre-speech recitation is believed to be especially potent because it imprints the day's first moment of consciousness with the auspicious vibrations of the thirteen names. The stotra is also recited on Monday (Somvar — the day of Shiva), Pradosha (the 13th lunar day morning and evening), Maha Shivaratri (the supreme Shiva festival), Shravan Somvar (the four Mondays of the Shravan month), Sankashti Chaturthi, and during the entire Shravan and Karthika months. Many South Indian Shaiva families recite it as part of the morning bed-prayer before the Karadarshana mantra. The brevity (3 verses, under one minute) makes it accessible to even the busiest devotee. (Source: Shiva Purana phala-shruti; Shaiva nityopasana tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What benefits does the Trayodash Shiv Naam grant according to tradition?
seoFaqs.answer: The phala-shruti (verse 3) of the Trayodash Shiv Naam promises four classical blessings to morning recitation: (1) Sarva-duhkhaih vinirmuktah — release from all sorrows, sufferings and miseries (mental, physical and emotional), (2) Sarva-siddhi-samanvitah — endowment with every siddhi (the eight Mahasiddhis: anima, mahima, laghima, garima, prapti, prakamya, ishitva, vashitva, plus the supernatural and worldly siddhis), (3) Dirgham-ayur-avapnoti — attainment of long life with health and vitality, and (4) Sarvan kaman avapnuyat — fulfilment of all desires (legitimate worldly desires, righteous wishes and ultimately the supreme desire for moksha). Tradition further attributes: pacification of Shiva-related grah doshas (Saturn, Rahu, Ketu, Mars), removal of obstacles in marriage, business and progeny, protection from akala-mrityu (untimely death) and apa-mrityu (accidental death), cure of mental afflictions and chronic disease, and ultimately Shiva-loka-prapti at the end of life. (Source: Trayodash Shiv Naam phala-shruti, Shiva Purana.)

seoFaqs.question: How does the Trayodash Shiv Naam differ from the Shiva Ashtottara and Shiva Sahasranama?
seoFaqs.answer: All three are Shiva-namavali stotras but differ in length and use. (1) The Shiva Sahasranama (1008 names of Shiva) is preserved in multiple recensions — Mahabharata Anushasanaparva, Shiva Purana, Linga Purana, Skanda Purana — and is the most comprehensive Shiva nama-koshah, recited during major Shiva-pujas, Rudrabhisheka and Maha Shivaratri vigils, taking 30-45 minutes. (2) The Shiva Ashtottara Shata Namavali (108 names of Shiva) is recited daily during the Bilva-archana — offering bilva leaves one per name — taking 5-7 minutes. (3) The Trayodash Shiv Naam (13 names) is the supreme short-form for the busy householder — completing in under one minute, recitable from bed on waking, requiring no preparation, no asana, no offerings — yet bestowing the same fundamental Shiva-kripa promised in the longer namavalis. The Trayodash Shiv Naam is therefore the gateway nama-japa for those who wish to begin daily Shiva upasana but cannot manage the longer formats. (Sources: Shiva Purana; Mahabharata Anushasanaparva 17.)

seoFaqs.question: Why are these specific thirteen names chosen out of the thousand names of Shiva?
seoFaqs.answer: The thirteen names selected for this stotra are not arbitrary — they collectively encode all the principal aspects of Shiva-tattva: the three philosophical aspects (Shiva as the Auspicious, Maheshvara as the Great Lord, Shambhu as Bliss-source); the iconographic forms (Pinaki with the bow, Shashi-shekhara with the moon-crest, Kapardi with matted hair, Shulapanih with the trident, Neelalohitah blue-throated, Virupaksha three-eyed); the five faces (Vamadeva — the western face); the cosmological functions (Bhava — source of existence, Shankara — peace-maker, Bharga — sin-destroyer). The number thirteen (trayodasha) is also significant because the thirteenth lunar day (Trayodashi / Pradosha) is sacred to Shiva, when Shiva-Parvati grant darshan during sandhya. Reciting the thirteen names on rising essentially aligns the devotee with the entire Shiva-tattva at the most receptive moment of the day. (Source: Shaiva nityopasana tradition; Trayodash Shiv Naam corpus.)
